stocks.discussion_driver: Adobe's board approved a new $25 billion stock repurchase program through April 2030, lifting shares ~2-3% on April 22, as the company attempts to restore investor confidence after a ~60% decline from its 2024 peak. The buyback follows CEO Shantanu Narayen's March 2026 announcement that he will step down once a successor is named — the company's first leadership transition in 18 years — amid mounting fears that AI-native tools from Canva, Google, and OpenAI are eroding Adobe's creative software dominance.
bull_case: At ~13x trailing P/E with an RSI of 29 (deeply oversold territory), Adobe trades at its most attractive valuation in years despite ~89% gross margins and ~$9.3 billion in annual free cash flow; the $25B buyback (~25% of current market cap) signals management's conviction in intrinsic value and provides meaningful EPS accretion at current depressed prices.

Adobe's Firefly AI platform is showing accelerating commercial traction, with Firefly Enterprise new customer acquisition growing 50% year-over-year; an Nvidia partnership and deep integrations across Creative Cloud and Document Cloud reinforce that AI features are becoming an incremental revenue driver rather than a pure competitive threat.
bear_case: AI-native competitors — Canva, Midjourney, and OpenAI's image and video generation tools — continue commoditizing creative workflows, threatening Adobe's ability to sustain subscription pricing power and average revenue per user growth, particularly in the mid-market and prosumer segments.

CEO Narayen's pending departure after 18 years creates leadership uncertainty at a critical strategic inflection point; the stock has lost ~60% from its 2024 all-time high, and Wall Street consensus remains Hold with a wide target range ($220–$510), reflecting deep disagreement over Adobe's AI monetization potential.

stocks.discussion_driver: L3Harris reports fiscal Q2 2026 earnings after market close today (April 22, 2026), a closely watched event as stalled U.S.-Iran ceasefire negotiations on April 21 sent defense names broadly higher on elevated geopolitical risk. Investors are focused on execution within the company's record backlog, margin trends on fixed-price development programs, and any update on the planned Missile Solutions unit IPO targeted for H2 2026.
bull_case: L3Harris's record multi-year backlog — built on expanding missile defense, ISR, and night-vision programs including a ~$465 million next-generation night-vision contract — provides exceptional revenue visibility as U.S. and NATO allies drive global defense budgets toward nearly $1.5 trillion by 2027, supporting the company's 7% organic revenue growth guidance for 2026.

Strong earnings momentum (+22.3% YoY EPS growth, +30.6% YoY free cash flow) and consistent EPS beats in three of the past four quarters demonstrate that the LHX NeXt cost-savings program is delivering real margin expansion; 19 of 24 analysts rate the stock Buy with a consensus target of $392.16.
bear_case: A significant portion of L3Harris's backlog involves fixed-price development contracts where cost overruns could meaningfully compress operating margins; dependence on large government program funding also creates revenue timing risk if U.S. defense appropriations face sequestration or prioritization shifts.

The stock already trades near its 52-week high at ~43x trailing P/E, leaving limited margin for error; if U.S.-Iran tensions de-escalate or Q2 guidance disappoints, the defense premium embedded in the current valuation could unwind sharply.
